original: Segðu þat, Eldir, áðr þú einn fet fetir framar: hvat hér inni at öl-málum dæma dýr regin?

translations.bellows: Speak now, Eldir, ere one step further thou farest: what speak the sons of the glorious gods at their ale within the hall?
translations.thorpe: Say, Eldir! ere thou footest one step forward, what the gods are talking of within, at their ale-drinking?
translations.literal: Say that, Eldir, before you one foot foot [step] further: what here in-side at ale-talks judge [discuss] precious powers?

original: Of vápn sín dæma ok of vígrisni sína dýr regin; Ása ok Alfa, er hér inni eru, manngi er þér í orði vinr.

translations.bellows: Of their weapons they speak and their prowess in war, the sons of the glorious gods; of the Aesir and Elves who are here within, none has a friendly word for thee.
translations.thorpe: Of their weapons they talk, and of their war-deeds, the glorious gods. Of the Aesir and the Alfs, who are here within, no one has a friendly word for thee.
translations.literal: Of weapons theirs judge and of battle-fame theirs precious powers; Aesir and Elves, who here in are, no-one is to-you in word friend.

original: Inn skal ek ganga Ægis hallir í, á þat sumbl at sjá; jöll ok áfu fæ ek Ása sonum, ok blanda ek þeim meini mjöð.

translations.bellows: In shall I go into Aegir's hall, for the feast I fain would see; bale and hatred I bring to the gods, and their mead with vice I mix.
translations.thorpe: In I will go into Ægir’s hall, to see the feast. Strife and hatred I will bring to the Aesir’s sons, and with their mead will mix malice.
translations.literal: In shall I go Aegir's halls in, on that feast to see; noise and insult get I to-Aesir's sons, and blend I to-them harm-mead.

original: Veiztu, ef þú inn gengr Ægis hallir í, á þat sumbl at sjá, hrópi ok rógi ef þú eys á ginnregin, á þér munu þau þerra þat.

translations.bellows: Know, if thou goest to Aegir's hall, the feast for to fain see, if with slander and spite thou sprayest the gods, on thee shall they wipe it off.
translations.thorpe: Knowest thou, if thou goest into Ægir’s hall, to see the feast, if with insults and ribaldry thou sprinklest the gods, they will wipe it off on thee.
translations.literal: Know-you, if you in go Aegir's halls in, on that feast to see, shout and slander if you pour on high-powers, on you will they dry that [wipe it off].

original: Veiztu þat, Óðinn, ef vit tveir deilum hrópi ok rógi hér, at ek mun þér verða mál-snjallari, ef þú vilt við mik mæla?

translations.bellows: Knowest thou, Odin, if we two contend with spiteful words here, that I shall be found more fluent of speech, if thou wilt strive with me?
translations.thorpe: Knowest thou, Odin! if we two contend with ribald words, that I shall be more ready with my answers, if thou wilt speak with me?
translations.literal: Know-you that, Odin, if we two deal shout and slander here, that I will to-you become word-cleverer, if you will with me speak?

original: Muntu þat muna, Óðinn, er vit í árdaga blöndum blóði saman? Ölvi deila léztu þér eigi mundu, nema vit værim báðir borit.

translations.bellows: Rememberest thou, Odin, in olden days that we both our blood have blended? Then didst thou swear no ale to quaff unless for us both it were brought.
translations.thorpe: Rememberest thou, Odin! when we in early days blended our blood together? Drink thou saidst thou wouldst not taste, unless it were for both of us presented.
translations.literal: Will-you that remember, Odin, when we in early-days blended blood together? Ale to-deal [share] said-you to-yourself not would, unless we were both [it] borne.

original: Rístu þá, Víðarr, ok lát ulfs föður sitja sumbli at, sízt oss Loki hrópi í Ægis höll.

translations.bellows: Rise up then, Vidar, and let the wolf's father sit at the feast; lest Loki speak words of shame to us here in Aegir's hall.
translations.thorpe: Rise then, Vidar! and let the wolf’s father sit at the feast, that Loki may not utter words of insult to us in Ægir’s hall.
translations.literal: Rise then, Vidar, and let wolf's father [Loki] sit feast at, since us Loki shouts in Aegir's hall.

original: Heilir Æsir, heilar Ásynjur ok öll ginn-heilög regin, nema sá einn Áss, er innar sitr, Bragi, bekkjum á.

translations.bellows: Hail to the Aesir! Hail to the Asynjur! Hail to all the holy gods! save only the god who sits within, Bragi, there on the bench.
translations.thorpe: Hail to the Aesir! Hail to the Asynjur! and all the holy gods! save that one Ás, who sits within, Bragi, on the bench.
translations.literal: Hail Aesir, hail Asynjur and all high-holy powers, except that one God, who inner sits, Bragi, benches on.

original: Hest ok hring gef ek þér af hoddum mínum, ok bót muntu Braga mæla, sízt þú vilt við regin reiðask; gerska-at þú, Loki, goðum.

translations.bellows: A horse and a ring from my hoard I give thee, and a better reward shalt thou find from Bragi, if thou wilt not be wroth with the gods; make not the gods angry, Loki.
translations.thorpe: A horse and a ring I will give thee from my hoard, and with a gift Bragi will atone, if thou wilt not be hostile to the gods. Anger not the gods against thee, Loki!
translations.literal: Horse and ring give I you from hoards mine, and compensation will-you Bragi speak, since you will against powers be-angry; make-not-angry you, Loki, gods.

original: Hests ok hrings muntu æ vana vera, Bragi, beggja þinna; Ása ok Alfa, er hér inni eru, þú ert við víg varastr ok skjarrastr við skot.

translations.bellows: Horse and ring shalt thou never have, Bragi, though both are thine; of the Aesir and Elves who are here within, thou art most fearful of fight, and shiest of shot.
translations.thorpe: Of horse and rings thou wilt ever be destitute, Bragi! Of the Aesir and the Alfs, who are here within, thou art the most afraid of war, and shiest of shot.
translations.literal: Horse and ring will-you ever lacking be, Bragi, both yours; Aesir and Elves, who here in are, you are with battle most-wary and most-shy with shot.

original: Veit ek, ef ek úti væra, svá sem ek inni em, í Ægis höll, höfuð þitt bæra ek í hendi mér; þat ek þér firi lúgi legg.

translations.bellows: I know if I were without as I am within, here in Aegir's hall, thy head in my hand I would bear, and for thy lie would pay thee.
translations.thorpe: I know, if I were without, as I am within Ægir’s hall, thy head I would bear in my hand; that should be thy reward for thy lying.
translations.literal: Know I, if I out were, so as I in am, in Aegir's hall, head your bore I in hand mine; that I to-you for lie lay.

original: Snjallr ertu í sessi, skreyta muntu-at, Bragi, bekkskraut; út þú gakk, ef þú reiðr sér, vaskr maðr vegr-at sér.

translations.bellows: Brave art thou in thy seat, but little thou'lt do, Bragi, the bench's pride; go forth and fight if angry thou art, for the brave man thinks not of fear.
translations.thorpe: Sitting thou art brave, Bragi! but thou shalt not do so, thou bench-ornament! Go out and fight, if thou art angry; a brave man sits not considering.
translations.literal: Brave are-you in seat, adorn will-you-not, Bragi, bench-ornament; out you go, if you angry are, brave man fights-not [for] himself [meaning: brave men don't just sit there].'

original: Bið ek þik, Bragi, barna-sifjum ok öllum ósk-mögum, at þú Loka kveðir-at löst-stafi í Ægis höll.

translations.bellows: I pray thee, Bragi, for our children's sake, and the sake of all our sons, that thou Loki speak not words of shame here in Aegir's hall.
translations.thorpe: I pray thee, Bragi! by our children, and all our adopted sons, that thou to Loki utter not words of insult in Ægir’s hall.
translations.literal: Ask I you, Bragi, by-children-kin and all wish-sons, that you Loki speak-not fault-staves in Aegir's hall.

original: Þegi þú, Iðunn, þik kveð ek allra kvenna ver-gjarnasta vera, sízt þú arma þína lagðir ítr-þvegna um þinn bróður-bana.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Idunn! of all women I say thou art most lustful; since thy arms so bright were wound about thy brother's slayer.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Idunn! of all women I say thou art the most wanton; since thou thy arms, carefully washed, didst throw around thy brother’s slayer.
translations.literal: Silent you, Idunn, you say I of-all women man-eagerest [most wanton] to-be, since you arms yours laid bright-washed around your brother's-slayer.

original: Loka ek kveð-at löst-stafi mæla í Ægis höll; Braga ek kyrri öl-reifan, vil-at ek, at it vreiðir vegizt.

translations.bellows: I speak not words of shame to Loki in Aegir's hall; Bragi I quiet, who is merry with ale; I would not that you two should fight.
translations.thorpe: To Loki I will not utter words of insult in Ægir’s hall. Bragi I will quiet, who is over-excited with ale. I would not that ye two should fight.
translations.literal: Loki I speak-not fault-staves in Aegir's hall; Bragi I quiet ale-cheerful, will-not I, that you-two angry fight.

original: Þegi þú, Gefjun, því at ek mun þat nefna, er þik glapði at geði: sveinn inn hvíti, er þér sigli gaf, ok þú lagðir lær yfir.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Gefjun! for now I shall tell who led thee to evil; the youth so bright gave thee a gift, and about him thy legs were wound.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Gefjun! for I will now mention how that fair youth lured thee to his mind. He gave thee a necklace, and thou didst throw thy legs around him.
translations.literal: Silent you, Gefjun, because that I will that name, what you lured at spirit: boy the white, who to-you jewel gave, and you laid thigh over.

original: Villr ertu, Loki, ok viti skertr, er þú Gefjun gørir þér vreiða; því at jarðar örlög hygg ek, at hón öll viti jafngörla sem ek.

translations.bellows: Mad art thou, Loki, and little of wit, to make Gefjun angry; for the fate of all she knows, I ween, even as well as I.
translations.thorpe: Mad thou art, Loki! and of wit bereft, when thou makest Gefjun angry; for the destiny of all the world I think she knows as well as I.
translations.literal: Wild [mad] are-you, Loki, and wit sheared [bereft], when you Gefjun make for-yourself angry; because that earth's fate think I, that she all knows equally-clearly as I.

original: Þegi þú, Óðinn, þú kunnir aldregi deila víg með verum; oft þú gaft, þeim er þú gefa skyldir-at, inum slævurum sigr.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Odin! thou never couldst guide the fate of men in the fight; oft hast thou given to him who deserved it not, to the less brave, victory in battle.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Odin! thou couldst never distribute victory among men. Oft hast thou given to those to whom thou oughtest not, to the more cowardly, victory.
translations.literal: Silent you, Odin, you could never deal battle among men; often you gave, to-those who you give should-not, the sluggish victory.

original: Veiztu, ef ek gaf, þeim er ek gefa skylda-at, inum slævurum sigr: átta vetr vartu fyr jörð neðan, kýr mólkandi ok kona; ok hefir þú þar börn borit, ok hugða ek þat args aðal.

translations.bellows: Knowest thou, if I gave to him who deserved it not, to the less brave, victory? Eight winters thou wast 'neath the earth, a cow-milker and a woman; and there didst thou bear children, and that I thought the work of a pervert [argr].
translations.thorpe: Knowest thou, if I gave to those to whom I ought not, victory? Eight winters thou wast under the earth, a cow-milker and a woman; and there didst thou bear children, and that I thought the work of a pervert [argr].
translations.literal: Know-you, if I gave, to-those who I give should-not, the sluggish victory: eight winters were-you before [under] earth beneath, cow milking and woman; and have you there children borne, and thought I that of-pervert [argr] nature.

original: En þig síðu skóku Sámseyju í, ok draptu á vétt sem völur; vitka líki fórtu ver-þjóð yfir, ok hugða ek þat args aðal.

translations.bellows: And they say that thou on Samsey once didst work with charms like a witch [völva]; in a sorcerer's guise didst thou travel among men, and that I thought the work of a pervert [argr].
translations.thorpe: But they say that thou in Samsey didst beat on the drum like a witch [völva]; in a sorcerer's guise didst thou wander among men, and that I thought the work of a pervert [argr].
translations.literal: But you side-shook [practiced seidr] Samsey in, and struck on lid [drum] as seeresses [volur]; wizard's likeness went-you man-folk over, and thought I that of-pervert [argr] nature.

original: Frigg kveð ek Fjörgyns dóttur, at hón sé ver-gjörnust vera, sízt þú, Vei ok Vilja, lézt þér ver í faðm taka, Óðins konu.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Frigg! thou art Fjorgyn's daughter, and ever hast lusted for men; for Vili and Ve, the brothers of Vithrir, both in thy arms have lain.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Frigg! thou art Fjörgynn's daughter, and hast ever been most wanton; since Ve and Vili, it is said, thou, Odin’s wife, didst into thy embrace take.
translations.literal: Frigg call I Fjorgyn's daughter, that she be man-eagerest [most wanton] being, since you, Vei and Vilja, let you man in embrace take, Odin's woman.

original: Veiztu, ef ek inni ætta í Ægis höllu Baldri líkan bur, út þú né kvæmir frá Ása sonum, vreiðr værir þú þá vegin.

translations.bellows: If a son I had here in Aegir's hall like Baldr who lately died, thou shouldst never come forth from the Aesir's folk till thy fierceness in fight were tried.
translations.thorpe: Knowest thou, if I had in Ægir’s hall a son like Baldr, out thou shouldst not go from the Aesir’s sons: thou shouldst be slain in thy anger.
translations.literal: Know-you, if I in-side had in Aegir's hall Baldr-like son, out you never come from Aesir's sons, angry were-you then slain.

original: Senn viltu, Frigg, at ek fleira telja mína mælstafi; því ræð ek, at þú Baldrs hrópir-at, sízt þú veizt, at ek því veld, at þú ríðat meir Baldr til sala.

translations.bellows: Shall I tell thee more, Frigg, of my wicked words? At my will shall it be; for I did the deed that Baldr no more thou shalt see riding home to the hall.
translations.thorpe: But wilt thou, Frigg! that I yet more mention of my evil words? It was my counsel that Baldr no more thou shalt see ride to the hall.
translations.literal: Soon will-you, Frigg, that I more tell my speech-staves; therefore rule I [I ensure], that you Baldr-shout-not [don't complain about Baldr], since you know, that I that cause, that you ride-not more Baldr to halls.

original: Feikn ertu, Loki, er þú fræja ræðr yðra illa stafi; Freyja ek kveð-at löst-stafi mæla í Ægis höll.

translations.bellows: Mad art thou, Loki, that here thou shalt tell the Aesir of all thy shame; for the fate of all Freyja knows, I ween, even as well as I.
translations.thorpe: Thou art frantic, Loki! in mentioning your foul deeds. Freyja I believe to be of wit bereft, since she makes thee angry.
translations.literal: Frightful are-you, Loki, when you news rule your ill staves [evil words]; Freyja I speak-not fault-staves speak in Aegir's hall.

original: Þegi þú, Freyja, þik kveð ek fælna vera ok meini blandaða; Ása ok Alfa, er hér inni eru, hverr hefir þinn hór verið.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Freyja! for fully I know thee, sinless thou art not thyself; of the Aesir and Elves who are here within, each has as lover been thine.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Freyja! I know thee full well; thou art not free from vices. Of the Aesir and the Alfs, who are here within, every one has been thy paramour.
translations.literal: Silent you, Freyja, you call I frantic [lustful] to-be and harm-blended; Aesir and Elves, who here in are, each has your lover been.

original: Fals er þér á tungu, hygg ek, at þér verði-at gott; vreiðir eru þér Æsir ok Ásynjur, hryggr muntu heim fara.

translations.bellows: False is thy tongue, and soon shalt thou find that it works thee weary woe; the Aesir are wroth, and the Asynjur all, and in grief shalt thou homeward fare.
translations.thorpe: False is thy tongue; I think that hereafter it will perform no good for thee. Wroth with thee are the Aesir and the Asynjur; home thou shalt go sorrowing.
translations.literal: Falsehood is to-you on tongue, think I, that to-you becomes-not good; angry are to-you Aesir and Asynjur, wretched will-you home go.

original: Þegi þú, Freyja, þú ert fordæða ok meini blönduð, sízt þig at bræðr þínum stóðu blíð regin ok þú munt þá, Freyja, frussa.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Freyja! thou witch [fordæða] art, and evil is ever thy mind; for with thy brother the gods all caught thee once, and then, Freyja, thou didst fart.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Freyja! thou art a sorceress, and with much evil blended; since against thy brother the beneficent powers found thee, and then, Freyja! thou didst break wind.
translations.literal: Silent you, Freyja, you are witch [fordæða] and harm-blended, since you with brother your stood kind powers and you will then, Freyja, [fart/sputter].'

original: Lítit er þat, þótt mær fái sér ver eða hór; hitt er undr, er Áss ragr kemr hér inn, er börn hefir borit.

translations.bellows: Small is the sin if a woman seeks a lover or husband to have; but a wonder it is that a god [ragr] should come who has borne him children here.
translations.thorpe: It is a small matter if a maiden gets herself a husband or a paramour; but it is a wonder that a wretched [ragr] Ás should here enter, who has children borne.
translations.literal: Little is that, though maid gets herself man or lover; this is wonder, when god unmanly [ragr] comes here in, who children has borne.

original: Þegi þú, Njörðr, þú vart austr heðan gísl um seldr goðum; Hymis meyjar höfðu þik at hland-trogi ok þér í munn migu.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Njord! from the east wast thou sent as a hostage to the gods; and the daughters of Hymir as a chamber-pot used thee, and into thy mouth they pissed.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Njord! thou wast from hence east-ward sent as a hostage to the gods. Hymir’s daughters had thee for a urinal, and in thy mouth made water.
translations.literal: Silent you, Njord, you were east from-here hostage about sold [given] to-gods; Hymir's maids had you for urine-trough and to-you in mouth urinated.

original: Sú er bót, er ek var austr heðan gísl um seldr goðum, at ek gat mög þann, er mangi hatar ok þykkir sá Ása prýði.

translations.bellows: This help I had when from the east I was sent as a hostage to the gods: I begat a son who is hated of none, and is thought the pride of the gods.
translations.thorpe: This is my comfort, as I was from hence east-ward sent as a hostage to the gods, that I begat a son, whom no one hates, and who is thought the prince of the Aesir.
translations.literal: That is compensation, when I was east from-here hostage about sold to-gods, that I got son that, whom no-one hates and seems that Aesir's pride.

original: Hættu nú, Njörðr, haf þú í hófi þik, ek mun því lengr leyna-at; við systur þinni gaztu slíkan mög, ok er-at þó vánu verra.

translations.bellows: Stop now, Njord! in bounds hold thyself, no longer the secret I'll hide; with thy sister a son so mighty thou hadst, and no worse than one would ween.
translations.thorpe: Cease now, Njord! in bounds contain thyself; I will no longer keep it secret: it was with thy sister thou hadst such a son, although he is not worse than was to be expected.
translations.literal: Stop now, Njord, have you in moderation yourself, I will that longer hide-not; with sister yours got-you such son, and is-not though than-expectation worse.

original: Auðigr ertu, Njörðr, ok áttu vé mörg, en þú hefir-at í Ægis höllu Baldri líkan bur, ok muntu æ vana vera.

translations.bellows: Rich art thou, Njord, and many a shrine thou hast, but thou hast not in Aegir's hall a son like Baldr, and ever shalt thou lack him.
translations.thorpe: Rich thou art, Njord! and many a temple hast; but thou hast not in Ægir’s hall a son like Baldr, and shalt ever be destitute of him.
translations.literal: Rich are-you, Njord, and have-you shrines many, but you have-not in Aegir's hall Baldr-like son, and will-you ever lacking be.

original: Þegi þú, Týr, þú kunnir aldregi semsa tveim um dæma; hægri hönd ek mun hinnar geta, er þér Fenrisúlfr sleit.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Tyr! for a right hand never couldst thou guide for two; thy right hand I shall name, that which Fenris-wolf tore from thee once.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Tyr! thou couldst never settle a foot between two. Of thy right hand I will mention, which Fenrir from thee tore.
translations.literal: Silent you, Tyr, you could never reconcile two about judging; right hand I will that get [mention], which to-you Fenris-wolf tore-off.

original: Handar em ek vanr, en þú Hróðvitnis, böl er beggja þrá; úlfrinn ok eigi hefir-at gott, er í böndum bíðr ragnarök.

translations.bellows: My hand I lack, but Hrodvitnir's son thou lackest, and bale to us both is the loss; the wolf is in chains, and in grief he bides the day when the gods shall go.
translations.thorpe: I of a hand am wanting, but thou of the wolf Hródvitnir; to us both the loss is cause of longing. The wolf has not fared well, who in bonds bides the gods’ destruction.
translations.literal: Hand am I lacking, but you Hrodvitnir [the wolf], bale is of-both longing; wolf-the also not has good, who in bonds bides Ragnarok.

original: Þegi þú, Týr, þat varð þinni konu, at hón átti mög við mér; alin né pening hafðu þess aldregi, vanréttis, vesall.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Tyr! for thy wife a son by me did happen to have; not a penny of pay or a cubit of cloth hadst thou for the wrong, thou wretch.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Tyr! it happened to thy wife to have a son by me; nor an ell nor a penny didst thou ever receive in compensation for that injury, thou wretch!
translations.literal: Silent you, Tyr, that happened to-your wife, that she had son with me; cubit nor penny had-you of-that never, of-wrong-done, wretched.

original: Freyr ek kveð-at löst-stafi mæla í Ægis höll; valla mundu þér Æsir ok Ásynjur vreiðir vera.

translations.bellows: I speak not words of shame to Freyr in Aegir's hall; surely the Aesir and Asynjur would not be wroth with thee.
translations.thorpe: To Freyr I will not utter words of insult in Ægir’s hall. Scarcely would the Aesir and the Asynjur be wroth with thee.
translations.literal: Freyr I speak-not fault-staves in Aegir's hall; scarcely would to-you Aesir and Asynjur angry be.

original: Þegi þú, Freyr, þú gaft Gýmis meyju gull ok seldir þitt sverð; en er Múspells synir ríða Myrkvið yfir, veizt-at þú, vesall, hvé þú vegr.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Freyr! for thy sword thou gavest for Gymir's daughter to have; but when Muspell's sons o'er Myrkwood ride, thou shalt lack a weapon, thou wretch.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Freyr! thou gavest Gymir’s daughter gold, and didst sell thy sword; but when Muspell’s sons through Myrkvið ride, thou shalt not know, wretch! how thou shalt fight.
translations.literal: Silent you, Freyr, you gave Gymir's maid gold and sold your sword; but when Muspell's sons ride Mirkwood over, know-not you, wretched, how you fight.

original: Mantu þat, Loki, er þú meinstafi ræðr yðrum illa stafi? Freyr ek kveð-at löst-stafi mæla í Ægis höll.

translations.bellows: Rememberest thou, Loki, when thou didst speak words of shame to the Aesir here? Freyr I speak not words of shame to in Aegir's hall.
translations.thorpe: Rememberest thou, Loki! when thou to the Aesir didst utter foul words? Freyr I believe to be of wit bereft, since he makes thee angry.
translations.literal: Remember-you that, Loki, when you harm-staves rule your ill staves? Freyr I speak-not fault-staves speak in Aegir's hall.

original: Þegi þú, Freyr, þú vart í Vanaheimi gísl um seldr goðum; en er þú vart í skógum sleginn, þá varst-at þú Baldri líkr.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Freyr! for in Vanaheim wast thou sent as a hostage to the gods; and when thou wast beaten in the woods, then wast thou not like Baldr.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Freyr! thou wast in Vanaheim as a hostage given to the gods; but when thou wast in the woods beaten, then wast thou not like Baldr.
translations.literal: Silent you, Freyr, you were in Vanaheim hostage about sold to-gods; but when you were in woods beaten, then were-not you Baldr like.

original: Ef ek átta eign, sem Freyr hefir, ok svá sæligh sali, ek munda-at láta þenna löst-stafi mæla í Ægis höll.

translations.bellows: If I had a heritage such as Freyr has, and so fair a hall, I would not let this speaker of shame speak words of shame in Aegir's hall.
translations.thorpe: If I had a heritage like Freyr, and so fair a hall, I would not let this utterer of foul words talk in Ægir’s hall.
translations.literal: If I had property, as Freyr has, and so happy halls, I would-not let this fault-staves speak in Aegir's hall.

original: Munaðu, Loki, er þú mæltir meinstafi yðrum illa stafi? Freyr ek kveð-at löst-stafi mæla í Ægis höll; Byggvir mælti, er hann í eyra heyrði Braga ok Loka deila.

translations.bellows: Rememberest thou, Loki, when thou didst speak words of shame to the Aesir here? Freyr I speak not words of shame to in Aegir's hall; Byggvir spoke, when he heard Bragi and Loki contending.
translations.thorpe: Rememberest thou, Loki! when thou to the Aesir didst utter foul words? Freyr I believe to be of wit bereft, since he makes thee angry. Byggvir then spake, as he in his ear heard Bragi and Loki contending.
translations.literal: Remember-you, Loki, when you harm-staves rule your ill staves? Freyr I speak-not fault-staves speak in Aegir's hall; Byggvir spoke, when he in ear heard Bragi and Loki deal [dispute].'

original: Veiztu, ef ek ætta öðli, sem Freyr hefir, ok svá sæligh sali, korn-mali mýra ek munda-at láta þenna löst-stafi mæla í Ægis höll.

translations.bellows: Knowest thou, if I had a heritage like Freyr's, and so fair a hall, I would grind thee to dust, thou speaker of shame, and speak words of shame in Aegir's hall.
translations.thorpe: Knowest thou, if I had a heritage like Freyr, and so fair a hall, I would crush this speaker of foul words, and speak words of shame in Ægir’s hall.
translations.literal: Know-you, if I had heritage, as Freyr has, and so happy halls, corn-grinder of-moors [Byggvir] I would-not let this fault-staves speak in Aegir's hall.

original: Hvat er þat it litla, er ek þat löggja sé ok snapvíst snapa? At eyru Freys muntu æ vera ok undir kvernum klaka.

translations.bellows: What is that little thing that I see there, cringing and sniffing about? At Freyr's ears shalt thou ever be, and tattle beneath the millstone.
translations.thorpe: What is that little being that I see there, fawning and snapping? At Freyr’s ears thou wilt ever be, and under the millstone chatter.
translations.literal: What is that the little, that I that wagging see and snap-wise snap? At ears of-Freyr will-you ever be and under millstones chatter.

original: Byggvir ek heiti, en mik bráðan kveða goð öll ok gumnar; hróðugr ek em þess, er hér drekka allir sínn öl-mál saman.

translations.bellows: Byggvir I am named, and nimble I am called by gods and men alike; proud am I of this, that the sons of the gods all drink their ale together.
translations.thorpe: Byggvir I am named, and nimble I am called by gods and men alike. Proud I am of this, that the Aesir’s sons all drink their ale together.
translations.literal: Byggvir I named-am, and me nimble call gods all and men; proud I am of-that, that here drink all their ale-talk together.

original: Þegi þú, Byggvir, þú kunnir aldregi deila mat með mönnum; ok þig í fleti fela kveða, þá er vágu verar.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Byggvir! thou never couldst guide the food for men in the fight; and hidden in the straw didst thou lie, they say, when men were at the battle.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Byggvir! thou never couldst distribute food among men. In the straw of the floor they say thou didst hide thyself, when men went to battle.
translations.literal: Silent you, Byggvir, you could never deal meat among men; and you in floor hide say [they], when fought men.

original: Þegi þú, Heimdallr, þér var í árdaga þat it ljóta líf of lagit: aurgu baki þú skalt æ vera ok vaka vörðr goða.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Heimdall! for thee in olden days was an evil fate decreed; with a stiff back thou shalt ever be, and wake as the watchman of the gods.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Heimdall! for thee in early days an evil life was fixed. With a wet back thou must ever be, and wake as the gods’ watchman.
translations.literal: Silent you, Heimdallr, to-you was in early-days that the ugly life of laid [decreed]: muddy back you shall ever have and wake warder of-gods.

original: Ölr ertu, Loki, svá at þú ert örviti, hví né lezk-at-tu, Loki? Því at ofdrykkja veldr alda hveim, er sína mælti-at mælgi.

translations.bellows: Drunk art thou, Loki, and mad thou art, why wilt thou not leave off, Loki? For over-drinking leads every man to speak what he should not speak.
translations.thorpe: Drunk thou art, Loki! thou hast lost thy wits. Why dost thou not leave off, Loki? For over-drinking leads every man to speak what he should not speak.
translations.literal: Drunk are-you, Loki, so that you are witless, why never leave-off-you, Loki? Because that over-drinking causes of-men each, that his spoke-not speech [speaks what he shouldn't].'

original: Þegi þú, Skaði, þú vart í skógum slegin ok áttu-at föður þinn; nú muntu æ vana vera, sízt þú lézt þér ver í faðm taka, Óðins konu.

translations.bellows: Be silent, Skadi! for in the woods wast thou beaten, and thy father thou shalt not have; and ever shalt thou lack him, since thou didst let Odin's wife in thy arms be taken.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, Skaði! thou wast in the woods beaten, and thy father thou shalt not have. Ever thou shalt lack him, since thou didst let Odin’s wife into thy embrace take.
translations.literal: Silent you, Skadi, you were in woods beaten and had-not father your; now will-you ever lacking be, since you let you man in embrace take, Odin's woman [Frigg].'

original: Léttari muntu í máli, ef þú á lopt berr; en þó muntu æ vana vera, sízt þú lézt þér ver í faðm taka, Óðins konu.

translations.bellows: Lighter of speech shalt thou be if thou goest forth; but ever shalt thou lack him, since thou didst let Odin's wife in thy arms be taken.
translations.thorpe: Lighter thou wilt be in speech, if thou goest forth; but ever thou shalt lack him, since thou didst let Odin’s wife into thy embrace take.
translations.literal: Lighter will-you be in speech, if you in air bear [go forth]; but though will-you ever lacking be, since you let you man in embrace take, Odin's woman.

original: Veiztu, ef þú á lopt berr, hvat ek þér mæla mun: á því krossi, er ek þér kveða mun, þú skalt æ vera.

translations.bellows: Know, if thou goest forth, what I shall tell thee: on the cross that I shall name for thee, there shalt thou ever be.
translations.thorpe: Knowest thou, if thou goest forth, what I will to thee say? On that cross which I will mention to thee, thou shalt ever be.
translations.literal: Know-you, if you in air bear, what I to-you speak will: on that cross [frame], which I to-you say will, you shall ever be.

original: Fyrir því ek þér mæla mun, sízt þú lézt þér ver í faðm taka, Óðins konu.

translations.bellows: Therefore I shall tell thee, since thou didst let Odin's wife in thy arms be taken.
translations.thorpe: Therefore I will to thee say, since thou didst let Odin’s wife into thy embrace take.
translations.literal: Because of-that I to-you speak will, since you let you man in embrace take, Odin's woman.

original: Veiztu, ef þú mæla muntu, hvat ek þér svara mun: þú vart fyrri mér á véum ok vildir mik vega.

translations.bellows: Know, if thou wilt speak, what I shall answer thee: thou wast first with me at the shrines and wouldst have killed me.
translations.thorpe: Knowest thou, if thou wilt speak, what I will answer thee? Thou wast first with me at the temples, and wouldst have slain me.
translations.literal: Know-you, if you speak will, what I to-you answer will: you were earlier [before] me at shrines and would me slay.

original: Öl muntu mæla, ef þú á Ægis bekki sitr ok vilt við mik mæla.

translations.bellows: Ale shalt thou speak, if thou sittest on Aegir's benches and wilt speak with me.
translations.thorpe: Ale thou wilt speak, if thou on Ægir’s benches sittest, and wilt with me speak.
translations.literal: Ale will-you speak, if you on Aegir's benches sit and will with me speak.

original: Veiztu, ef þú á Ægis bekki sitr ok vilt við mik mæla, hvat ek þér svara mun: þú vart fyrri mér á véum ok vildir mik vega.

translations.bellows: Know, if thou sittest on Aegir's benches and wilt speak with me, what I shall answer thee: thou wast first with me at the shrines and wouldst have killed me.
translations.thorpe: Knowest thou, if thou on Ægir’s benches sittest, and wilt with me speak, what I will answer thee? Thou wast first with me at the temples, and wouldst have slain me.
translations.literal: Know-you, if you on Aegir's benches sit and will with me speak, what I to-you answer will: you were earlier me at shrines and would me slay.

original: Heilir Æsir, heilar Ásynjur ok öll ginn-heilög regin, nema sá einn Áss, er innar sitr, Loki, bekkjum á.

translations.bellows: Hail to the Aesir! Hail to the Asynjur! Hail to all the holy gods! save only the god who sits within, Loki, there on the bench.
translations.thorpe: Hail to the Aesir! Hail to the Asynjur! and all the holy gods! save that one Ás, who sits within, Loki, on the bench.
translations.literal: Hail Aesir, hail Asynjur and all high-holy powers, except that one God, who inner sits, Loki, benches on.

original: Hví þú þá, Loki, mælisk af gólfi firi? sess þú á bekki bera! þá skal freista, hvárr fleira viti, gestr eða inn gamli þulr.

translations.bellows: Why then, Loki, dost thou speak from the floor? Take thy seat on the bench; then shall we try who knows more, the guest or the ancient sage.
translations.thorpe: Why then, Loki! speakest thou from the floor? Take thy seat on the bench. Then shall it be seen which knows the more, the guest or the old talker.
translations.literal: Why you then, Loki, speak from floor before? seat you on bench bear! then shall [we] try, which more knows, guest or the old sage.

original: Þegi þú, rægishríð, þér munu mínar hendr hamri loka; herðu-kamb ek þér ór höfði lýst, svá at þér verðr hvert bein brotið.

translations.bellows: Be silent, thou speaker of shame! for my hands shall stop thy mouth with the hammer; I shall strike the bone-comb from thy head, so that every bone in thee shall be broken.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, thou ribald! my hands shall stop thy mouth with the hammer. I will strike thy bone-comb from thy head, so that every bone in thee shall be broken.
translations.literal: Silent you, slander-storm [Loki], to-you will my hands with-hammer lock; shoulder-comb [head] I from-you from head strike, so that to-you becomes each bone broken.

original: Jarðar burr er hér nú kominn; hví þú svá, Þórr, mælisk? En þú þorir eigi, þá er þú skalt við úlf vega ok svelgr hann Alföður allan.

translations.bellows: The son of Earth has now come here; why dost thou speak so, Thor? But thou wilt not dare when thou shalt fight with the wolf, and he swallows the All-father whole.
translations.thorpe: Jörd’s son is now come here. Why dost thou so, Thor! speak? But thou wilt not dare, when thou shalt fight with the wolf, and he devours the All-father whole.
translations.literal: Earth's son [Thor] is here now come; why you so, Thor, speak? But you dare not, when you shall with wolf fight and swallows he All-father all [entirely].'

original: Þegi þú, rægishríð, þér munu mínar hendr hamri loka; upp ek þér kasta í austrveg, síðan þik mangi sér.

translations.bellows: Be silent, thou speaker of shame! for my hands shall stop thy mouth with the hammer; up shall I throw thee into the eastern regions, and no one shall see thee again.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, thou ribald! my hands shall stop thy mouth with the hammer. I will cast thee up into the eastern parts, so that no one shall see thee more.
translations.literal: Silent you, slander-storm, to-you will my hands with-hammer lock; up I you throw in east-way, since you no-one sees.

original: Of austrförum þínum skaltu aldregi segja seggjum frá, sízt þú í hanzka-fingri hnúðir, einherji, ok þóttist-at þú Þórr vera.

translations.bellows: Of thy journeys to the east thou shalt never tell men; for in the thumb of a glove didst thou crouch, thou great fighter, and thoughtest thou wast not Thor at all.
translations.thorpe: Of thy journeys to the east thou shalt never tell to men; since in a glove-finger thou didst crouch, thou great warrior! and didst not think thyself to be Thor.
translations.literal: Of east-journeys yours shall-you never say to-men from [about], since you in glove-finger crouched, champion, and thought-not-you you Thor to-be.

original: Þegi þú, rægishríð, þér munu mínar hendr hamri loka; ek slæ þér af lofthvílum, svá at þú kemst aldri aftr til Ásgarðs.

translations.bellows: Be silent, thou speaker of shame! for my hands shall stop thy mouth with the hammer; I shall strike thee out of the air, so that thou shalt never come back to Asgard again.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, thou ribald! my hands shall stop thy mouth with the hammer. I will strike thee out of the air, so that thou shalt never more come back to Asgard.
translations.literal: Silent you, slander-storm, to-you will my hands with-hammer lock; I strike you from air-rests [legs/existence], so that you come never back to Asgard.

original: Þegi þú, rægishríð, þér munu mínar hendr hamri loka; herðu-kamb ek þér ór höfði lýst, svá at þér verðr hvert bein brotið.

translations.bellows: Be silent, thou speaker of shame! for my hands shall stop thy mouth with the hammer; I shall strike the bone-comb from thy head, so that every bone in thee shall be broken.
translations.thorpe: Be silent, thou ribald! my hands shall stop thy mouth with the hammer. I will strike thy bone-comb from thy head, so that every bone in thee shall be broken.
translations.literal: Silent you, slander-storm, to-you will my hands with-hammer lock; shoulder-comb [head] I from-you from head strike, so that to-you becomes each bone broken.

original: Kvað ek fyr Ásum, kvað ek fyr Ása sonum, þat er mik lysti at mæla; en fyr þér einum mun ek út ganga, því at ek veit, at þú vegr.

translations.bellows: I have spoken to the Aesir, I have spoken to the sons of the Aesir, what my mind did list to say; but before thee alone shall I go forth, for I know that thou wilt strike.
translations.thorpe: I have said before the Aesir, I have said before the Aesir’s sons, what my mind suggested; but before thee alone I will go out, because I know thou wilt strike.
translations.literal: Spoke I before Aesir, spoke I before Aesir's sons, that which me listed [desired] to speak; but before you alone will I out go, because that I know, that you strike.

original: Öl görðir þú, Ægir, en þú aldri munt síðan sumbl gera; eiga þín öll, er hér inni er, leiki yfir logi, ok brenni þér á baki.

translations.bellows: Ale hast thou brewed, Aegir, but nevermore shalt thou make a feast; all thy possessions here within shall the fire play over, and burn thy back.
translations.thorpe: Ale thou hast brewed, Ægir! but thou shalt never more a feast provide. All thy possessions, which are here within, may the flame play over, and burn thy back!
translations.literal: Ale made you, Aegir, but you never will since feast make; property your all, which here in is, play over flame, and burn to-you on back.

original: En eftir þetta faldist Loki í Fránangrsforsi í lax líki. Þar tóku æsir hann.

translations.bellows: But after this Loki hid himself in Franangr's waterfall in the shape of a salmon. There the Aesir caught him.
translations.thorpe: After this Loki hid himself in Fránangr’s waterfall in the likeness of a salmon, where the Aesir took him.
translations.literal: But after this hid-himself Loki in Franangr's-waterfall in salmon's likeness. There took Aesir him.

original: Hann var bundinn með þörmum sonar síns, Nara; en Narfi sonr hans varð at vargi.

translations.faulkes: He was bound with the entrails of his son, Nari; and Narfi his son became a wolf.
translations.brodeur: He was bound with the guts of his son Nari; but his son Narfi was changed into a wolf.
translations.literal: He was bound with guts of-son his, Nari; but Narfi son his became at [into] wolf.

original: Skaði tók eitruðu orm ok festi upp yfir andlit Loka; draup þar ór eitr.

translations.bellows: Skadi took a venomous serpent and fastened it up over Loki's face; the venom dripped down from it.
translations.thorpe: Skaði took a venomous serpent, and fastened it up over Loki’s face. The venom dropped from it.
translations.literal: Skadi took venomous serpent and fastened up over face of-Loki; dripped there from poison.

original: Sigyn, kona hans, sat þar ok hélt mundlaug undir eitrit. En er mundlaugin var full, þá gekk hón út at hella ór eitrinu; en á meðan draup eitrit á Loka.

translations.bellows: Sigyn, his wife, sat there and held a basin under the venom. But when the basin was full, she went out to pour out the venom; and meanwhile the venom dripped on Loki.
translations.thorpe: Sigyn, his wife, sat by him, and held a dish under the venom. When the dish was full, she went out to pour out the venom; but in the meantime the venom dropped on Loki.
translations.literal: Sigyn, woman his, sat there and held basin under venom-the. But when basin-the was full, then went she out to pour from venom-the; but in meanwhile dripped venom-the on Loki.

original: Þá kipptist hann svá hart við, at jörð öll skalf; þat eru nú kallaðir landskjálftar.

translations.bellows: Then he jerked so hard that the whole earth trembled; those are now called earthquakes.
translations.thorpe: Then he writhed so violently that all the earth shook: those are what are now called earthquakes.
translations.literal: Then jerked-himself he so hard with, that earth all shook; that are now called land-shakes [earthquakes].'

original: Þar liggr hann í böndum til ragnarök.

translations.bellows: There he lies in bonds until Ragnarok.
translations.thorpe: There he lies in bonds until Ragnarök.
translations.literal: There lies he in bonds until Ragnarok.
